skill.name: threejs-skills
skill.description: Guides AI assistants in creating browser-based 3D scenes, WebGL interactions, animations, and visual effects with Three.js.
skill.summary: Create responsive Three.js scenes, animations, interactions, and WebGL visualizations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are Markdown prose, JavaScript examples, or inline code references, not executable shell or cryptographic code. The only real concern is a third-party cdnjs import example for Three.js, which is a supply-chain dependency but not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Third-Party CDN Dependency in Example Import
low_findings.description: The skill recommends importing Three.js from cdnjs in generated browser examples. This is a common demo pattern, but it creates a dependency on an external host and should be pinned or replaced for production use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 28 contains a direct HTTPS import from cdnjs. The context is legitimate Three.js setup guidance, so the risk is supply-chain exposure rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command matches are Markdown code fences and inline JavaScript identifiers inside documentation. No shell execution, Ruby backticks, subprocess calls, or user-input command construction were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences or JavaScript examples in a single documentation file. There are no executable support files or commands that would run on install or use.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance findings are triggered by normal English words and Three.js rendering guidance. The reviewed lines discuss interactive graphics, lighting, camera clipping, and feature compatibility.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found prose and rendering terminology, not crypto APIs, password hashing, host discovery, or system inspection commands. No prompt injection language was found in the searched skill content.

content.value_statement: Creating reliable browser 3D scenes requires correct Three.js setup, rendering loops, lighting, and responsive behavior. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for WebGL scenes and interactive 3D effects.
actual_capabilities: Creates baseline Three.js scenes with scene, camera, renderer, geometry, materials, and lights.
Adds animation loops with requestAnimationFrame for rotating objects and visual effects.
Implements mouse movement, click selection, hover states, wheel zoom, and simple camera controls.
Guides particle systems, shadows, fog, reflections, custom geometry, and grouped objects.
Provides troubleshooting guidance for black screens, missing lighting, visibility errors, and performance issues.
Explains when to use CDN demos versus production builds with modern tooling.
limitations: The main examples target Three.js r128 from a CDN, so newer APIs may be unavailable.
It does not include reusable asset files, model files, or a complete application scaffold.
Advanced loaders and post-processing are discussed at a high level, not fully implemented.
Generated scenes still require browser testing for performance, framing, and visual quality.

use_cases.title: Prototype a 3D Demo
use_cases.description: Quickly create a rotating object, product preview, particle field, or interactive browser scene.

use_cases.title: Visualize Spatial Data
use_cases.description: Convert data points, relationships, or abstract concepts into animated 3D scenes.

use_cases.title: Add Portfolio Visual Effects
use_cases.description: Build polished WebGL effects with lighting, camera motion, shadows, and responsive behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a simple Three.js scene with a rotating cube, basic lighting, and responsive resizing.

prompt_templates.title: Add User Interaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Three.js scene where users can drag to rotate the camera, scroll to zoom, and click objects to change their appearance.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data Visualization
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an animated 3D visualization for a dataset, with labeled objects, camera movement, and performance-conscious geometry choices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Production Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production-ready Three.js architecture using modern imports, model loading, animation sequencing, resource disposal, and performance optimization.

output_examples.input: Create a rotating 3D sphere that follows the mouse.
output_examples.output: A browser scene plan with a perspective camera, standard material, ambient and directional lights, mouse tracking, smooth rotation, and resize handling.

output_examples.input: Make a particle background for a landing page.
output_examples.output: A performance-aware particle system design with a limited point count, subtle motion, camera placement, and responsive renderer sizing.

output_examples.input: Troubleshoot why my Three.js canvas is black.
output_examples.output: A focused checklist covering scene objects, camera position, render loop execution, material lighting needs, and browser console errors.

best_practices: Start with scene, camera, renderer, one visible mesh, and one render loop before adding complexity.
Use lighting only when the chosen material requires it, and verify visibility after each change.
Dispose of geometries, materials, and textures when removing objects from long-running scenes.
anti_patterns: Do not use Three.js APIs that are unavailable in the selected version without checking compatibility.
Do not add large particle counts, high segment geometry, or heavy effects before testing frame rate.
Do not rely on external assets or CDN imports for production without version pinning and review.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete Three.js applications?
faq.answer: It provides strong scene patterns and implementation guidance. Full application structure still depends on the project framework.

faq.question: Which Three.js version does it emphasize?
faq.answer: The main examples use r128 from a CDN for simple browser demos and artifact compatibility.

faq.question: Can it help with mouse and click interaction?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers drag controls, wheel zoom, mouse tracking, raycasting, click selection, and hover effects.

faq.question: Can it load 3D models?
faq.answer: It discusses production loading patterns with GLTFLoader, but the main CDN examples focus on built-in geometry.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production work?
faq.answer: It is best for foundations and prototypes. Production projects should use package imports, asset review, and performance testing.

faq.question: What security concern remains?
faq.answer: The main concern is the recommended external CDN import. Production users should pin dependencies and review external asset sources.
